data.title: SoundHound AI: Murky Growth
data.description:
SoundHound AI, Inc. (NASDAQ:SOUN) remains one of the most misunderstood AI stocks. The voice AI business has no organic growth, yet the stock trades at a premium valuation. My investment thesis remains ultra Bearish on the investment story, though the market is likely to continue overpaying for SoundHound AI due to the misleading reported growth rates.

No Organic Growth

SoundHound AI remains a perplexing stock, with the market constantly ignoring the lack of organic growth. The company hyped voice AI growth for drive-thru ordering and in-car voice commerce, yet the business never took off.

Instead, SoundHound AI bought Amelia for ~$200 million in combined stock and assumed debt and reported these combined results, including $3.0 million for SYNQ3, in Q1 '24 as follows:

The company only ended up reporting Q1 '25 revenues of $29.1 million. While the reported growth rates were impressive at 151% over the original $11.6 million reported last Q1, the new SoundHound AI actually had pro-forma sales of $36.0 million.

SoundHound AI presented a picture of fast growth, but the company hasn't really explained what happened to revenues when $6.9 million disappeared in the process of integrating Amelia. At the start of 2024, management had forecast 2025 revenues topping $100 million prior to the Amelia deal, and now the guidance is only $167 million despite adding a business with somewhere around $100 million in sales back in 2024 and the company reporting $143 million in pro-forma 2024 revenues.

The SYNQ3 deal promised a restaurant pipeline of 100,000 locations. The deal was suppose to provide a $25 million orderbook to voice AI ordering with the combined company having 10,000 active locations when closing the deal in Q1 '24.

The company definitely offers a lot of promise in AI, whether via agentic AI from the Amelia side or voice AI via SoundHound AI. So many large tech companies offer agentic AI solutions one really has to wonder if a company just willing to cash out for a stock valuation below $100 million really has a leading product.

SoundHound paints a picture of strong growth with statements regarding adding 1,000 restaurants to the voice ordering business in Q1 to reach 13,000 locations now. In fact, the CFO painted a picture of growth on the Q1 '25 earnings call, which isn't what is actually occurring below the surface:

Q1 revenue was $29.1 million, up 150% year-over-year. The revenue base continues to meaningfully diversify, with no single customer contributing more than 10% of our revenue in the quarter. This is much improved from historical levels, provides overall risk mitigation, and reflects our expanded serviceable market. We are seeing strong year-over-year growth in our customer service offerings in particular, driven by location expansion in restaurants and scaling across the enterprise business.

A prime example is that Q2 '25 consensus forecasts are for sales of just $33 million in the June quarter, while pro-forma sales were $34 million last year. The company is just producing a few million bump up from the low point in the prior quarter while relying on SoundHound AI to reach a massive Q4 sales total of $53 million in order to hit the yearly target.

The Amelia acquisition closed on August 6, so all of the revenues reach organic sales in the next few weeks. The big question is how sales are going to soar from $33 million in Q2 to $53 million in Q4, when sales actually slipped sequentially in the December quarter last year.

Priced For Perfection

The company guided to 2025 revenues of $157 to $177 million, suggesting some organic growth, but the numbers don't add up. SoundHound AI had pro-forma 2024 revenues of $143 million, so the lower end of guidance offers limited growth for 2025 while the stock trades as if the AI company actually has 150%+ organic growth.

The stock has a market cap of nearly $5 billion with 414 million shares outstanding, trading at nearly 30x sales targets with questionable organic growth. Even if SoundHound hits the sales targets for 2025 and starts growing organically at a 20% clip, the stock is already priced for growth exceeding 50%.

SoundHound AI ended the quarter with cash of $246 million and burned $19.2 million from operations in Q1 alone. The company reported an adjusted EBITDA loss of $22 million and continues moving further away from being profitable.

A trader might want to own the stock on a technical breakout from the recent resistance around $12.50. The stock quickly doubled the last time this level was breached, but SoundHound AI jumping up to 50x to 60x sales again seems far fetched, though highly possible.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that SoundHound AI has very murky growth due to the complicated comparisons from major acquisitions. The guidance for Q3/Q4 should start waking the market up to the lack of organic growth, while the management team hasn't fully explained how negative pro-forma sales growth will suddenly turn into big growth rates in Q4.

Investors should sell the stock at a very premium valuation.

data.title: Gaia: Niche Leadership In A Great Industry But Can't Seem To Leverage Its Growth
data.description:
Gaia Inc. (NASDAQ:GAIA) owns a spirituality and wellness streaming service, with almost 900 thousand subscribers.

The service has been consistently growing for years, and most content is produced in-house, making the company a leader in its niche.

The company is interesting because of its niche leadership, growth, adjacent opportunities, and potentially leverageable operations. However, it has so far proved unable to generate profitability from its growth, and it would take a very long time to leverage to the profitability needed to justify its current market cap in my view.

For that reason, I initiate the name with a Hold.

Business description

Gaia's main business is to operate a streaming service exclusively focused on wellness and spirituality.

Its content mixes several niches that sometimes intersect and sometimes do not, including yoga, tai chi, nutrition, alternative medicine, meditation, astrology, mysticism, alternative history, pseudoscience, and others.

The company does not disclose how much of its library belongs to each of the categories, nor which are consumed the most by its users. When doing reviews online (or if readers have seen the services' advertising), the content that surfaces the most is related to the most esoteric categories, but this might be only because that kind of content is more exotic compared to yoga or vegetarianism.

The important factor is that the company enjoys a relatively strong audience of almost 900 thousand subscribers, and growing at a healthy CAGR of more than 15% since the service launched in its present form in 2016. The company is still adding subscribers and revenues at 10%+ in 1Q25.

Positive economics

There are several aspects that make me think Gaia's business is very interesting, not the least of which is the growth shown above.

The company's growing media library (in several languages and 75%+ produced in-house, according to its 10-K) generates a moat against competitors, because a new user has a lot of content to watch. Gaia amortizes the content very fast, at less than 4 years on average, on CoGS. Therefore, although the content has a balance sheet value of $40 million, its gross unamortized value (undisclosed in the financials) might be much larger. In a recent presentation, the company disclosed a replacement value of $180 million.

The company also enjoys the adjacent business opportunities that stem from a very niche community. It can introduce other products. For example, the subsidiary company Igniton sells supplements, or the company has organized spirituality trips to Egypt or Peru (1Q25 call). The company's brand value in its niche might make it a very good negotiator with content producers and figures in the area, because appearing on Gaia is in itself a good way of advertising.

Strong balance sheet

GAIA has a strong balance sheet. The company recently issued about $7 million in equity (see 1Q25 cash flows from financing), probably to pay for a mortgage loan maturing this year, and now has about $7 million in net cash ($13 million minus $6 million in debt) on its balance sheet.

This is enough to sustain the company, given that it generates sufficient CFO to pay for investment in new content (the CFO of the company already incorporates media library investments) and for CAPEX, which includes licenses purchased from third parties plus PP&E. The result is operations at about breakeven FCF.

Controlling shareholder

The company's Chairman is also its founder and largest shareholder, with about a 23% beneficial ownership and 75% voting power, from the latest proxy. The Chairman was also the company's CEO until very recently, and the current CEO has worked for the company for 9 years.

I believe these are also strong factors, because they indicate that management and the board are aligned with shareholders.

Lack of operational leverage

Despite the above positive comments, the company has seemed unable to really leverage its cost structure after its initial growth period.

In particular, Gaia has an enormous reliance on advertising to keep a growing customer base (and replace churning users). Advertising made up about 40% of revenues in the past fiscal years, and in the company's model financials (provided in the latest investor presentation, page 12 under 'Revenue Benchmark Scenarios - Pro Forma'), the company considers advertising as a variable figure, and therefore not leverageable in my opinion.

I believe the main reason why advertising cannot be leveraged is that the company experiences a high level of churn, which means it needs to invest most of the advertising budget in replacing or resubscribing users, rather than on pure growth.

We also do not observe leverage on the content library amortization. The company's gross margins have remained more or less flat, with content amortization representing about 7% of revenues consistently.

To provide some contrast, observe how Netflix's (NFLX) EBITDA margins consistently grow, even to this day, and especially as the company moved to a full streaming service in the early 2010s.

Valuation is not attractive without leverage

The main problem with GAIA as an investment is that, without earnings leverage, I believe there is no way to think of earnings growth or return to shareholders (in the form of dividends or buybacks).

With its level of growth (10%+ compounded for several years), Gaia should not have to generate a lot of profit to justify a $100 million market cap, maybe just as little as $5 million in net income would be okay, or even less. Especially if those earnings were leveraged, and we could think of a double engine of revenue and margin growth.

However, in its present form, growth cannot be considered a source of return because it is not translated into earnings. The company has considered in its investor presentation (page 13) that about 40/45% of its expenses (the operating ones) are operationally leverageable, but we have not seen this in reality, despite 20% growth in revenues since 2022, when the EBITDA margins stopped growing.

For that reason, in its present economics, I cannot justify an investment in Gaia, despite the niche leadership of the company and its (potential) very positive economics.

That's why I initiate the name with a Hold rating for the time being, but will continue to follow it with interest in future quarters to see a change in effective economics.

Not much to expect for 2Q25

I do not believe the 2Q25 figures will represent a surprise, given the low seasonality and recent stability of the company's results. It is normal for Gaia to grow about 2/3% sequentially, in both revenues and users.

Further, the company's margins have been, as mentioned, very stable over time. It would be very surprising (positively) to see EBITDA margins improving despite moderate revenue growth (on a quarterly basis, pretty high on a yearly basis).

The only factor that I think could cause some volatility is the depreciation of the USD against other currencies, as shown below for the Dollar Index. Because the company has about half of its subscribers abroad, and they probably pay their subscriptions in the local currency, when the dollar depreciates, those same subscriptions get converted to dollars.

Risks

I do not believe that Gaia presents too many risks. Quite on the contrary, it is probably its relative stability that gives it a valuation that I believe is excessive. The stability that Gaia enjoys is not as positive as I would prefer in order to invest in the name.

With this said, I believe Gaia is exposed to consumer discretionary forces, like any entertainment or media service. If people have less money to spend on things, they tend to cut down on non-essentials, and Gaia is probably one of them. However, the company has not shown in the past a great sensitivity to these cycles.

In addition, the company is exposed to the cultural cycle of growth of spirituality and wellness. If people became more secular, or some of the practices like yoga or astrology fell out of vogue, the company could suffer. However, I think these trends are secular, and they should take decades to play out. Further, the trend is currently in Gaia's favor.

Finally, and related to the stock itself, we should note that Gaia does not have high liquidity. The stock trades about 40 thousand shares per day, or $160 thousand at current prices. That is only 0.15% of shares traded per day. This means that prices can move very violently with surprises, or when the general market is volatile. This should be taken into account by anyone interested in taking a position on the name.

data.title: Broadcom: AI Boom And Innovation To Sustain Growth In The Long Run
data.description:
Thesis statement

I am bullish on Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:AVGO) given the massive market opportunity with the AI chip market projected to grow at a CAGR of 28.92% between 2024 and 2034, reaching a market volume of $927.76 billion. Broadcom is positioning itself in the chip sector by introducing new competitive chip solutions such as the Tomahawk Ultra. Tomahawk can tie together four times more processors to boost Ethernet speed. This enables it to perform at high speed and capacity for high workload. Tomahawk Ultra is currently the best viable alternative to Nvidia's high-speed graphics GPUs, NVLink, and NVLink Switch, making it a major leverage point for Broadcom to offer more solutions to Alphabet's Google.

Given this optimistic market outlook and Broadcom’s position to seize the chip market with new competitive chips, I will proceed to explore this in detail below to explain why I am bullish.

Company Brief

Broadcom Inc. is a designer, developer, and supplier of various semiconductor devices used in mixed-signal complementary metal-oxide semiconductors (CMOS) and complex digital semiconductors and analog III-V based products globally. The company operates in two segments: Infrastructure Software and Semiconductor Solutions. The company provides Ethernet switching, custom silicon solutions, and routing, as well as copper and optical physical layer devices, Wi-Fi access point SoCs, and fiber optic components. The company also provides cable modems, mobile device connectivity solutions, DSL/POL products, and set-top box SoCs. These products are used in various sectors, including AI networking, enterprise networking, home connectivity, data centres, telecommunications, factory automation, and smartphones, among other applications. The company was founded in 1961 and is headquartered in Palo Alto, California.

AI With Real Impact But Very Underutilized

I am examining the AI chip market through the lens of AI and Gen AI development. Overall, the AI market is scaling workers’ productivity and improving business efficiency. According to research conducted by McKinsey, more than 500 users of AI have shown that the use of AI reduced vehicle maintenance and operating costs in the transportation industry by 10%. Other sectors also report improved cost reductions, which is a major driver of profitability for most companies.

Even with this ambitious outlook, only 1% of company leaders describe their companies as mature in terms of AI deployment. As such, around 92% of companies are advocating that they are planning to increase their investment in AI.

To achieve a trillion-dollar market outlook by 2040, the market relies heavily on the semiconductor industry. For example, to deploy its full-scale competitive AI, a scalable AI infrastructure represents a new fundamental shift. In hindsight, scalable AI chips are a major focus in the next road to a trillion-dollar AI market outlook by 2040.

AVGO Riding The Market Tide

Looking at Broadcom’s comprehensive portfolio of semiconductors and software infrastructure solutions, its specialization in advanced Ethernet connectivity and switch technologies, it will likely seize the AI market from the chip side. The company is prioritizing the provision of chips for AI infrastructures and data centers, which is why I consider the Tomahawk 6 series to be Broadcom’s response to the AI sector's need for scalable and larger workload-capable chips. What I mean by this is that Broadcom is offering the Tomahawk Ultra, the world's first 102.4 TB/s switch available to meet the next phase of AI chip demand.

Now, given this projected demand, I will further investigate how Tomahawk compares with chips that offer similar capabilities to see its market desirability. A close competitor to the Tomahawk Ultra chip, which offers nearly the same capabilities as the Tomahawk Ultra, is Nvidia's NVLink Switch. Nvidia's NVLink Switch is a better option for large-scale GPU clusters, supporting up to 576 GPUs in a fully connected NVLink configuration. This makes NVLink suitable for HPC and AI applications, where large-scale parallel processing is needed from GPUs. NVLink is built to handle GPU-centered workload at scale. On the other hand, Broadcom’s Tomahawk Ultra surpasses NVLink in terms of bandwidth at 102.4 TB/s, which supports the interconnectivity between data centers in AI, cloud applications, and networking. This is what brings out the aspect of scalability. In contrast to parallel processing across GPUs using Nvidia’s NVLink, Tomahawk Ultra offers a high bandwidth of 102.4 TB/s and interconnectivity, supporting the large-scale distribution of AI workloads and providing scalability across data centers and multiple servers.

Therefore, my conclusive finding is that Nvidia’s NVLink is better suited for GPU-intensive AI and HPC workloads, while Broadcom matches the scalability demands of hyperscalers. Tomahawk Ultra's scalability for AI networks and high-performance networking in large-scale data centers makes it a reliable option for hyperscale data centers.

In my opinion, as the AI race intensifies, scalability is becoming inevitable, as evidenced by hyperscalers such as Amazon, Google, Meta, and Microsoft, which are seeking larger AI models to capitalize on the growing opportunities in AI. The hyperscalers and megacaps are planning to spend around $320 billion combined on AI and data center development by the end of 2025.

Broadcom is likely to be a winner among hyperscalers, given that it already has megacaps such as Apple, which represents 13.3% of Broadcom’s sales, and Samsung, which represents 21.3% of Broadcom's sales. The demand for scalable AI chips by the AI infrastructure is expected to drive Broadcom’s share of revenues to upwards of 40% by 2026 from AI chips. In conclusion, I expect the company's innovation to sustain its double digit revenue growth in the long run.

Financials, Projections & Valuation

Before doing my projections and valuations, I will reflect on this company’s top and bottom line trends over the last couple of years. Over the last five years, the company’s revenue has shown consistent upward trajectory. However, in 2023 its growth rate slowed down from what had been a consecutive two years of double digit growth. It grew by about 7.9% down from 20.96% the previous year and the effects were felt on the bottom line with its net income dipping between 2023 and 2024. However, the company has resumed a solid upward momentum both on its top and bottom line registering a 43.98% revenue growth in 2024 and 33.85% growth TTM.

The weak growth in 2023 was due to inventory issues in its non-AI networking with segments like the server storage connectivity seeing revenue slump of 17% and broadband a drop of 9%. Further, alongside the weak revenue demand in the year, net income dipped due to a onetime $4.5 billion non cash tax provision in Q3 2024 from an intra-group transfer of intellectual property to the US. Despite this short term setback, the company is demonstrating strong recovery with both the revenue and net income on a solid uptrend as demonstrated above and its gross and income margins being impressively high and on an uptrend this year.

I am looking at this current momentum against the company’s triple digit growth in its infrastructure software growth which seems to have picked momentum from lows of 2.54% in 2023 to above 100% recently. I believe this is testament that this company is riding the AI boom and it’s likely to sustain its current growth momentum since AI is still in its infancy stage and thus plenty of growth opportunities awaits.

With this background, I will estimate the company’s financials for the next few years. In my estimates, I assume a growth rate of 27% for 2025, lower than 2024 because the company picked from a lower growth rate in 2023. For the subsequent years, I am anticipating a consistent double digit growth in the region of 20%-25%. I think this strikes a balance between competition and growth opportunities. With my assumptions, I estimate revenue of about $145 billion by 2029.

My estimates are within range with consensus estimates on Seeking Alpha, which project revenue of about $127 billion by 2029.

With these projections, I will estimate my price target using the P/S ratio. Comparing this company’s PS of 23.02x with that of NVDA at 28.26x, it follows that this company is undervalued. While this could be attributed to NVDA’s superiority in its GPUs, I think AVGO current innovation taking NVDA head-on shows its growing innovative power. It is no surprise that the market has awarded AVGO a forward PS ratio of 21.02x above NVDA’s 20.87. This shows the high market expectation from AVGO. Assuming a forward PS of 20.5x, which is slightly conservative compared to the market’s 21.02x, I estimate a price target of $659.24 by 2029 as follows.

Investment risks

Global economic uncertainty

The chip sector is also facing uncertainties around tariffs and is a potential disruptor in the chip supply chain. This, in turn, risks Broadcom’s future revenue generation.

Government regulations

Increased trade tensions are leading to increased trade restrictions, which may potentially impact the overall efficiency of trade agreements that facilitate profitability.

Competition

The chip market is becoming increasingly competitive every day and hyperscalers are seeking affordable options such as designing their own chips. This risks Broadcom, potentially leading to the loss of customers in the future.

Conclusion

Although the stock is overvalued, I am reiterating my bullish rating due to Broadcom’s ability to design chips that offer scalability to hyperscalers at the time workload and speed are needed altogether. This provides a high revenue generation opportunity.

data.title: Evolution AB: Bullish Take Built Off Too Many 'What Ifs' In Q2 Results
data.description:
Above: Evolution AB (publ) (EVVTY) trending north after Q2 results reinforce the bull possibilities after price at writing of $79 USD. But overly bullish futures have hung around early trading values of nearly all sports betting stocks. Beware of the bulls now. This sector's trading pattern has been a carbon copy of initial bursts of highs after which we see major tanking.

Above: Evolution earnings growth continues into 2025 but is elusive.

  • Evolution's Q2 results encourage big bullish takes because they show a first time sequential increase across the key metrics. But caution: Sports betting is a low margin, high revenue growth business where two leaders combined (Flutter (FLUT) and DraftKings (DKNG)) still dominate 72% of the entire market.
  • Analysis of the bull outlook contains every what if bull trope that has been used by analysts (including myself on FanDuel--FLUT). There are caution flags raised as well, but they usual reside in the what if text.
  • Evolution AB has a long track record of software and operational success in sports betting, mitigated by deep concerns about Evolution's dips into grey area sites. However, in Q2, much of these worries have been struck off the stock by regulatory decisions and a healthy upside in revenue.

Source: Grandview Research.

Sports Betting Sector realities vs. Dreams: Evolution AB chances for crashing head on to the market share leadership in double digit shares are small. We saw many bullish takes spring loose after the Q2 numbers and nose dive. Whenever new sports betting platforms appear, their promoters always reach into the the speed lane in explaining the rocket ride of sports betting revenues that push these stocks way beyond their valuations. In citing the zooming growth of the sports and iGaming online since the SCOTUS 2018 ruling, we are subject to the same reiteration of its dynamics: Domination of U.S. market by two top platforms combining to produce 72/73% of the total revenue of the sector. (They are FanDuel and DraftKings, which together comprise an unchallenged 70+% of the entire market for sports betting).

BetMGM (MGM) (16% share of market) and Caesars Sportsbook (CZR) (8.5%) are the only other platforms that have reached double-digit market shares after massive marketing spends in giveaways and promos. We don't imply here that Evolution would shy away from these kind of marketing costs. But we do imply here that the 12 other USD platform from their origins to today have likewise poured hundreds of millions and some even billions into marketing and still have not left the overwhelming majority of platforms buried in single digit shares.

Most recent example: Since its debut around a year ago, mighty ESPN Bet (PENN) has remained stuck in low single-digit shares of market. So imagine the heft of ESPN national coverage, married to its massive daily audiences for programming and games and brand exposure in the U.S. miles above any competitor, unable to stretch beyond under a 3% share after a year. BetMGM has risen to double digits because it has driven growth with huge expenditures on marketing and live sports locations in its brick and mortar properties/ But overall, our discussions with operators in the field see a possible rise of MGM's betting online platform to 18 to 20%.

Source: Grandview Research.

Realistically, because of the early mover status of FanDuel and DraftKings' conversion of their big Fantasy Sports databases, they both had huge head starts in building their real money betting sites so that they have maintained their near 8-year leadership shares. Their over-70% share of the entire U.S. market should hold over the next five years. Growth in the sector from here on up will come from new legalizations by U.S. states. There are three now getting close to legalization. But also threats. It is rumored that President Trump's, recently passed BBB contains a national gaming tax.

As a former senior executive in the Trump casino business, I believe we will see a lot more back and forth on this tax before it actually winds up as a cost on the sports books tax accounts.

But assuming the national tax evades all efforts to get it on the books of the platforms, they will find a way to absorb the cost without a devastating blow to their operating margins. Most likely it could bury itself in subtle changes in the odds of certain bets, and the slimming down of promo richness. What investor will see is definitely a shot across the bow to all gaming operations--but not a killer blow. Trump has been part of the gaming industry insiders enough to understand how such a tax can be made least harmful.

Other than the possible impact of a national tax (in the BBB), we have the overhang of state-raised taxes, of which Illinois has already pushed on. Technological breakthroughs by given platforms in this sector are rarely enough to move market shares significantly. They can be copied and widely applied by every peer in a matter of months.

Above: 48% of all global Sports Betting comes from Europe and Asia.

These are the kind of tested elements or events that are most likely to keep the market share positions locked in where they currently are. Evolution AB will maintain a single-digit share that should keep its share of market now, pretty much locked in over the next few years. The sector is far too crowded already both in the U.S. and the globe. Existing leaders will continue to pour in resources to hold share. That leaves few options for the possible 10 to 20 other holders to see their small market shares as a service to bettors rather then a mainstream provider of big accretive profits

Conclusion

After the positive Q2 report, Evolution is going to enter the consciousness of investors as the next bright new penny in the cash drawer. Like all sports betting stocks, they'll get an immediate boost off the excessive bull analysis that follows. That could move the shares to new highs. But once we see following quarterly reports of slowing growth, the stocks will enter a series of dives over time, except for the locked in market share leaders, FLUT and DKNG.

So this is a cautionary tale from an inside observer both from the business itself and the sector. The two "musts" of your portfolio in sports betting should either be FLUT or DKNG, already sitting impregnable atop the sector. There is no fundamental change that will dramatically rattle market shares--except one. And that is a merger or acquisition of platforms that not only eliminates competitors but positions merged company to build market share.

Investors watching Evolution ADR who might be convinced it's a golden shot at a big payout downstream might find the upside enthusiasm is a melting ice cube after it's initial burst of bull scenarios exhaust themselves. This is the trail of most online gaming stocks every time.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Alcoa: A Company Between Tariff Pressures And Asset Sales, Time To Wait And See
data.description:
Alcoa Corp. (NYSE:AA) is one of the world's leading producers of primary aluminum ingots and sheets. At the same time, it is one of the oldest in the world and is a vertically integrated company engaged in bauxite mining, alumina refining, and energy production, all of which are key inputs for aluminum production.

Alcoa maintains a presence through its mines and factories in countries such as the United States, Brazil, Canada, Iceland, Norway, and Spain, among others. However, this geographic diversification and vertical integration, which was an advantage for the company in earlier stages, will not necessarily help Alcoa navigate the changes in the structure of international trade proposed by the new Trump administration. The change of administration in the White House in 2025 brought with it the possibility of sharp increases in import tariffs by the United States on countries that do not have free trade agreements with Washington.

Understanding the potential impact of these measures on the company will be key to determining when it might be a good opportunity to buy or exit. For now, Alcoa represents a company with significant advantages, as we'll see below, but it's mired in a context of many uncertainties that need to be resolved.

Thesis

To begin with the investment thesis, I would first like to describe the current aluminum market to understand the context in which Alcoa operates. This analysis aims to understand the drivers of global supply and demand, as well as the role of Washington's new tariff policy since Trump returned to the United States presidency.

First, to understand the market for molten and refined aluminum for industrial use, I find it interesting to analyze the industry's basic inputs. This will allow us to determine which goods to focus our analysis on when understanding the supply and demand processes. Regarding molten aluminum, as can be seen, alumina and energy are the main inputs. For alumina manufacturing, a process also carried out by Alcoa, we see that bauxite, energy, and chemicals are the main inputs. This is relevant since Alcoa not only operates as a producer of molten aluminum, but in an effort to generate vertical integration, the company also mines for bauxite extraction and maintains a business segment dedicated to the production of electrical energy for its factories.

As you can see, Alcoa's main mines are located in Brazil and Guinea, while its refineries and smelters are located in the United States, Canada, Europe, and Brazil. This allows the company to produce the essential raw materials for the final product, molten aluminum.

Before addressing the difficulties that changes in US international trade policy may bring, I would like to highlight the main drivers of demand and supply. On the demand side, as can be seen below, one of the drivers of increased demand for this product is expected to be driven by the change in the energy mix and the push for renewable energy.

On the supply side, a highly interesting situation is how supply might behave at current international aluminum prices. The main aluminum-producing country is currently China, with an annual production of 43 million metric tons. It is followed by India and Russia with 4.2 million and 3.8 million tons, respectively. This highlights the significant difference between Chinese and international production.

Now, as you can see, it is shocking to see that at the current international alumina price of $363/mt, 80% of Chinese refineries are unprofitable. This could have a significant impact on the supply of both alumina and aluminum.

Now, I want to analyze how international prices for both aluminum and alumina fluctuated. For alumina, the sharp rise in international prices was due to supply disruptions and a subsequent drop to $363/mt. Regarding international aluminum prices, the sharp declines were primarily due to the announcements of increased tariffs on imports by the American government.

That said, it's logical to wonder, if Alcoa has such efficient vertical integration as I mentioned earlier, why Trump's announcements regarding foreign trade are having a negative impact on Alcoa. As can be seen below, Canada and the Middle East are the main importing countries for the main inputs for aluminum production. Among Washington's announcements, the US would impose an increase in import tariffs to 50% on Canada.

This could undoubtedly have a significant negative impact, leading to a sharp increase in costs for the company. This would undoubtedly lead to an erosion of the company's margins. While this has not yet been reflected in the company's results, from my perspective, it is necessary to wait and see what impact these measures could have on Alcoa.

Sale of Ma'aden Assets

The last point I would like to comment on is the news that was released at the beginning of the month regarding the sale of 25.1% of the Ma'aden JV that Alcoa has jointly held with Saudi Arabian Mining Company for ~86 million Ma'aden shares valued at ~$1.2 billion and $150 million in cash.

Following the transaction, Alcoa retains a 2% stake in Ma'aden and expects to realize a $780 million gain in other income from this asset sale by 3Q2025. Under the agreement, Alcoa must hold Ma'aden shares for a minimum of three years and may sell them at most one-third of the shares per year.

2Q2025 Results

I would now like to briefly summarize the company's latest results for 2Q2025 and compare them with previous quarters. First, I'd like to mention the change in Alcoa's gross revenue last quarter. In 2Q2025, revenue totaled $3,018 million, a slight decrease compared to the $3,369 million reported in 1Q2025. During the quarter, there was a slight decrease in the price of LME aluminum, which closed at $2,592/mt, and a drop in alumina to $363/mt.

Now, if we analyze the company's Adjusted EBITDA, we observe a deepening trend. While the value reported in 1Q2025 was $855 million, 2Q2025 closed with a sharp drop in values of $313 million. This represents a significant 63.4% quarter-over-quarter decline. I find it interesting to note that, if we annualize this value and compare it to Alcoa's EV, which is around $8.85 billion, the EV/EBITDA ratio shows a result of 7.06x, which is not at all surprising in my opinion.

Turning to Net Income, we see a similar trend in Adjusted EBITDA, with a strong month-over-month increase. While it closed at $548 million in 1Q2025, it was $164 million in 2Q2025. This resulted in a decrease in EPS from $2.07 to $0.62.

If we analyze debt, the company's total reported amounts to $2.650 billion, to which we can add net pensions and EPEB liabilities. Now, when we break down the amount into short- and long-term debt, we see that the amount due next year amounts to $120 million, while long-term debt represents the remaining amount. Considering a cash position of approximately $1.51 billion, I see no short-term financial distress.

Valuation

In this segment, I'd like to show a DCF valuation I created to add some depth to the previous analysis. The idea I sought to capture in the model is to be able to capture the context I described in the thesis segment in its assumptions and understand whether there is any upside potential for the company worth exploiting. All of this while maintaining a margin of safety that, in my opinion, is acceptable, since the assumptions I sought to consider for the model, which I will describe below, sought a scenario that was not entirely favorable for Alcoa.

To continue with the analysis, I'll describe the assumptions I used in building the model. First, I used a 40% tax rate as the value for the calculations. If we analyze the effective tax rate in the SeekingAlpha Financials segment over the last few years, with a few exceptions, this value is above the average for recent years. On the other hand, taking into account the average growth of global GDP over the past few decades and the relationship between the growth of aluminum as an important metal in a very diverse range of industries, as I showed in the thesis section, I decided to use a perpetual growth rate of 2% per year.

Let's now analyze the company's WACC. Considering that Alcoa maintains a market capitalization of $7.61 billion and a debt stock of $2.66 billion. Below, we can see the bonds issued by the company, where we can see that the largest amount issued is concentrated in maturities between 2029 and 2031. Looking at the YTM of these bonds, we can see that the weighted average cost of debt is just over 6%. With that in mind, a Cost of Equity for this company of 12.65% seems quite reasonable from my perspective. Taking this into account, the WACC value obtained is 11%.

I'll now explain the assumptions related to EBIT and the other components that make up FCF. Regarding EBIT, I used the last twelve months' value displayed on SeekingAlpha as a reference. For the second year, I took into account a sharp decline in EBIT. I did this to take into account that the deepening impacts of the change in US foreign trade policy will affect the company throughout 2025 and will likely impact results in 2026.

From that initial decline, I proposed a return to the company's EBIT growth of 3.5% in the third year and 3% in the final two, ultimately converging to the perpetual growth rate. While there is the possibility of a strong recovery after the initial impact of the White House's tariff policy change, I decided not to take this into account to make the model more conservative.

On the other hand, it's important to keep in mind that Alcoa is a mature company, with an equally mature production process. Therefore, it's logical to assume that, barring any new acquisitions that require it, the amounts for depreciation and amortization, as well as CAPEX, will remain more or less constant and grow in line with production growth. In this regard, I assumed growth in depreciation and amortization of 2.5%, 2%, and 1.5% in last years. Regarding CAPEX, I assumed growth of 2% and 1%, remaining constant in last years.

With these assumptions, what can be seen is that the company is trading at almost Fair Value, without presenting much upside potential, at least using this method and at current prices.

One last point I'd like to mention is the company's reporting ratios. What can be seen is that the current multiples are below industry averages and, in many cases, are attractive. However, it's important to mention that the calculations are based on previous quarters' values. This doesn't take into account possible changes in the company's results in the face of tariff increases by the new Trump administration.

Risks

At this point, I would like to list the main risks that Alcoa could face. The main risk, which I have alluded to throughout the investment thesis, is the change in the foreign trade policy of the new Trump administration. As I mentioned, Canada and the Middle East are the main sources of aluminum raw material for the United States' subsequent processing. The White House had extended the deadline for establishing a free trade agreement between the various countries and the United States, something that could lead nations to avoid increased tariffs from Washington. However, so far, agreements have only been reached with the United Kingdom and Vietnam, and the deadline extension expires on August 1st.

At the time of writing this report, while negotiations are still ongoing, Canada has not signed a new agreement with the United States, so tariff increases on products such as steel, aluminum, and automobiles remain possible. Therefore, it will be important to see how this potential conflict is ultimately resolved and, if the tariff is finally implemented, what impact it could have on the company. It is clear that Alcoa's aluminum purchasing costs from customers outside the United States will increase significantly. From my perspective, the company will have a very difficult time avoiding the resulting margin squeeze this could generate.

There are products that, due to their high versatility, are strongly linked to global GDP growth. To name a few, we can mention copper, steel, and, of course, aluminum. Taking this into account, the possibility that the US tariff increase could trigger a trade war between different countries is not an overly low probability. One of the main targets of the measure would be China, with a tariff increase of 125%.

From my perspective, if the situation escalates, it could trigger a global recession that would consequently affect the increase in global demand for aluminum, in turn impacting production volumes and international prices more than we've already seen.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Alcoa's current situation is somewhat mixed. On the one hand, the 1Q2025 showed a significant increase compared to the previous quarter, in addition to the sale of assets to consolidate the company's capital structure. But that scenario was largely reversed with the latest results announced in 2Q2025. The debt stock does not appear to be a problem in the short and medium term. Finally, a series of multiples can be seen that, in my opinion, are highly attractive for companies like this. However, on the downside, the tariff increases by the United States undoubtedly compromise the company's cost structure.

The potential impact of this change on foreign trade could not only result in higher final prices paid by Alcoa for the purchase of inputs for the manufacture of industrial aluminum, but also in the event of a recession. If the escalation of the US-China trade war results in a decline in global GDP, this could likely lead to a drop in demand and, therefore, in aluminum sales prices. In this scenario, Alcoa would see its margins sharply reduced, both by an increase in its costs and by a drop in the international price of the product it produces.

Taking all this into account, I believe it's time to wait until I can see the balance tip. That's why I consider Alcoa a Hold.

data.title: July's 5 Dividend Growth Stocks With Yields Up To 7.96%
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

For some background on this monthly publication, here is my view on dividend growth stocks:

Dividend growth stocks aren't always the most exciting investments out there. They often aren't grabbing the headlines, and they aren't the stocks running up hundreds of percentages in a year. In fact, they are often some of the least exciting stocks. And that is precisely their strongest selling point. With such a vast world of dividend growth stocks available out there, it is important to screen through to see if there are any worthwhile investments to explore.

They are stocks that provide growing wealth over time to income investors. Dividend growers are often larger (not always), more financially stable companies that can pay out reliable cash flows to investors. Some are slower growers than others. Some are going to be cyclical that require a strong economy. Some are going to be secular, which doesn't generally rely on a more robust economy.

Dividend growth can promote share price appreciation. Of course, that is if these companies are growing their earnings to support such dividend growth in the first place. Trust me. There are yield traps out there - I've owned a few that I'm not particularly proud of.

I like to think of investing in dividend stocks as a perpetual loan of sorts. Essentially, every dividend is a repayment of your original capital. Eventually, holding long enough, you have the position "paid off." It is all returned back into your pocket from that point forward.

All of this being said, it is important to understand my approach to dividend stocks and why screening dividend stocks can be important for income investors. As with any initial screening, this is just an initial dive - more due diligence would be necessary before pulling the trigger.

The Parameters For Screening

I'll be using some handy features that Seeking Alpha provides right here on their website for this screen. In particular, I will be screening utilizing their quant grades in dividend safety, dividend growth and dividend consistency.

Dividend Safety is relatively self-explanatory. These will be stocks that SA quants show reasonable safety compared to the rest of their various sectors. The grade considers many different factors, but earnings payout ratios, debt and free cash flow are among these. This category will be stocks with A+ to B- ratings.

For the dividend growth category, we have factors such as the CAGR of various periods relative to other stocks in the same sector. Additionally, the quants also look at earnings, revenue and EBITDA growth. As we will see, this doesn't mean that every stock with a higher grade has the growth we are looking for. This just factors in that the dividend has grown or earnings are growing to support possible dividend growth. For these, the grades will also be A+ through B- ratings.

Finally, for dividend consistency, we want stocks that will be paying reliable dividends for us for a very long time. In particular, hopefully, they are raising yearly, though that isn't an explicit requirement. We will also include stocks with a general uptrend in dividend payments, which means there could have been periods where they paused increases for a year or two.

After looking at those factors alone, we are left with 423 stocks at this time, which is the exact same number listed last month. I'll link the screen here, though it is a dynamic list that constantly updates regularly. When viewing this article, there could be more or less when going to the link.

From there, I wanted to narrow down the list a lot more. I then sorted the list by TTM dividend yield, from highest to lowest. Since these will be safer dividend stocks in the first place, screening for those among the higher payers shouldn't hurt. Though there are always exceptions, after all, this is simply an initial screening, and I try to note those that I feel aren't as safe as the algorithms are suggesting.

As is usually the case, there are a couple of names that we'll skip over as they don't quite meet what we are looking for in this screening process. That is The Western Union Company (WU), which hasn't raised its dividend in years. Ennis (EBF) is also a newer name, but similar to WU, they haven't raised their payout in years aside from a large special paid last year. We'll also skip over Artisan Partners Asset Management (APAM) as they pay a variable dividend.

On top of that, there are several names that we also took a quick look at more recently, so while Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE), Flowers Foods (FLO), VICI Properties (VICI) and Lincoln National Corp (LNC) are all interesting names, we'll wait at least a quarter being touching on them again.

#1 RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ) 7.96% Yield

RLJ isn't a new name to the list, but it is one that only started screening highly back in November 2024, then has frequently been making the list since. This is a hospitality REIT with 96 hotels in 23 states. RLJ has seen its share price drop just over 20% in the last year, though it is off of the even lower share price levels we saw when we last took a look at the name in April.

Of course, everything dropped sharply in a very volatile April that we experienced when Trump announced retaliatory tariffs, which kicked off a wave of uncertainty. While the broader equity market quickly recovered, there were pockets that had not experienced the same lift. That includes RLJ, which remains down over 30% from its 52-week high.

With remaining depressed, that's continued to keep its dividend yield higher—even if it has come down a touch since our prior update. RLJ is quite sensitive to overall economic conditions, and if economists are expecting higher inflation, pressuring consumers further, then something like traveling for leisure could see a decrease. That could be bad news for a hospitality REIT that counts on visitors to fill their rooms.

They've been raising their dividend aggressively the last few years, but we can see how quickly that can change. During the Covid pandemic, they had nearly eliminated their dividend entirely, keeping just the token penny dividend.

The current payout ratio based on FY 2025 AFFO estimates comes in at a very modest 46%. However, we already saw AFFO decline in FY 2024, and according to estimates, FY 2026 isn't looking too rosy either.

This could be an interesting longer-term play for an investor who doesn't mind the increased risk of investing in a REIT in this space.

#2 United Parcel Service (UPS) 6.22% Yield

Speaking of another area of investing that is being disrupted and providing uncertainty, we have UPS. Naturally, when screening for higher yielders, there tend to be certain industries that tend to provide those higher yields. On the other hand, we have those that make the list due specifically because of broader pressures on their share price. As the share price drops, the yield climbs higher, assuming they don't cut their payout.

UPS has seen its share price drop almost 22% in the last year and is down nearly 29% from its 52-week high. Therefore, we are seeing UPS being in the latter camp of a company that doesn't typically see its dividend yield at these sorts of elevated levels, but the pressures in its industry. UPS has seen its earnings decline significantly for the last two years, and FY 2025 isn't expected to go any differently. That said, analysts are estimating that 2026 could eventually be the turning point where earnings could once again be on the rise.

UPS has raised its dividend for 15 consecutive years, and that includes the last two years, where they kept the streak alive despite the declining earnings. That said, it was the token penny raises that companies sometimes do during these sorts of downturns. In my opinion, keeping the dividend flat or these smaller raises is appropriate during times like this to keep their balance sheet healthier over the longer term.

There was also the massive dividend bump that they had a few years ago, which was after they saw earnings explode higher. Unfortunately, those explosive earnings we saw in FY 2021/2022 didn't last long.

#3 Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY) 5.21% Yield

BMY, unlike RLJ and UPS, has actually seen its share price rise by nearly 18% over the last year. However, it is still off its 52-week high by around 26%. Shares participated in the April downturn and have not seen a recovery. In fact, the healthcare sector overall has remained rather pressured and is one of the sectors down YTD, along with consumer discretionary.

Healthcare is often considered quite a defensive area of the market; however, we haven't really been seeing that play out at this time. One of the pressures is a new regulatory environment with RFK Jr really shaking up the space and providing uncertainty.

This isn't the first time we've given BMY a quick look, but it has been quite a while, as you'd have to go back to May 2024 for our last brief update. I generally view the large-cap healthcare companies, such as BMY, as fairly attractive due to their tendency to provide predictable earnings and predictable dividends. BMY has raised its dividend for 17 consecutive years now and has been paying a dividend for 35 years.

Pressuring BMY is that analysts aren't expecting any earnings growth going forward. In 2025, we technically see a massive increase from FY 2024, but there were one-time factors related to their Karuna acquisition and Systlmmune collaboration that pressured 2024 specifically. Using the $7.70 reached in 2022, it has otherwise been a downward trend since that peak.

Looking at FY 2025 estimates, the current dividend works out to a modest payout ratio of less than 37%. That should provide at least some cushion for the company to take its time to potentially return to earnings growth in the future.

At this time, I would view the healthcare space as rather attractive for a long-term investor. I already have a few positions in this space, such as Merck (MRK), AbbVie (ABBV) and Pfizer (PFE), as well as several funds that provide healthcare exposure specifically.

#4 NorthWestern Energy Group (NWE) 5.08% Yield

Similar to healthcare, the utility sector is seen as another area that is traditional defensive with predictable cash flows amid providing necessary services/products. In fact, utilities can be seen as even more predictable, but they can be quite sensitive to interest rate changes. Higher long-term rates make their yields relatively less attractive for income-focused investors, who can sell out of equity utility names and invest more in fixed-income for less risk.

Further, higher short-term and long-term rates can also pressure this space as the costs of borrowing can rise. This is an industry that naturally carries a heavier debt burden given their large infrastructure projects, but also the consistent business means that higher leverage ratios aren't seen as too problematic.

NWE is also not a new name to this list, and we last gave it a look in December 2024. This is a multi-utility company based in South Dakota. They've seen their earnings trending higher over the last couple of years, and analysts expect that to continue for the coming years.

That should bode well for the continued growth in the dividend, as they've been growing their dividend for 19 consecutive years now. Based on FY 2025 estimates, the current dividend payout ratio comes to around 73.7%. For some industries, that could be seen as really high. That is still on the higher end for utilities, but it isn't to an extreme extent or anything.

#5 T. Rowe Price Group (TROW) 5.08% Yield

TROW is a new name that we'll be touching on today, though most investors probably need very little introduction. This is a rather well-known asset manager—it isn't necessarily the largest asset manager, but at ~$1.6 trillion AUM, they are still sizable. Since the FY 2021 EPS peak for the company, they have been moving mostly sideways.

That hasn't kept them from continuing to raise their quarterly dividend, though. They've raised their dividend for 38 consecutive years, and I suspect that is a trend they hope to continue. However, at some point, it would seem that they'll need to find a way to generate some earnings growth rather than this somewhat flat trend.

With a payout ratio of around 61% going forward based on the latest quarterly dividend, they aren't necessarily at any sort of crisis level. However, they are on the higher end relative to their asset manager peers by a fairly high level. BlackRock (BLK), the world's largest asset manager, has a forward payout ratio of ~45% and State Street Corp (STT) is at around 38%, as a couple of examples.

These two larger peers have also shown a trend of generally rising earnings, helping to support dividend growth and some capital appreciation. While the current yield of TROW is quite appealing, I'd find some of its competitors more attractive at this time. STT, in particular, also shows a similar valuation in terms of the forward P/E multiple being around 11x.

data.title: Svenska Handelsbanken Q2 Earnings: Not Quite As Bad As It Looks
data.description:
Svenska Handelsbanken's (OTCPK:SVNLF) (OTCPK:SVNLY) second quarter financials were not well received by the market, as shares of the Stockholm-based bank are currently trading around 8% below their pre-results level. That has a driven a roughly similar performance since my last update in May, albeit 2025 has still been a good one for the ADSs, which have returned around 30% so far this year.

Turning back to Q2 results, they don't make for pretty reading at first glance. Revenue, net income and earnings per share were all down double-digits year-on-year, with Handelsbanken clocking in the worst performance of the Nordic banks I track.

While the market's response would suggest otherwise, I don't think last quarter was quite as bad as the headline figures suggest. And with the stock's recent tumble sending its dividend yield over the 12% mark, I think the value case for Handelsbanken remains attractive.

A Poor Set Of Headline Figures

Handelsbanken's headline numbers admittedly look pretty bad, with revenue of SEK 13.6 billion down around 12% year-on-year and 8% sequentially. Earnings also tumbled, with pretax pre-provision income (SEK 7.6 billion), net income (SEK 5.5 billion) and EPS (SEK 2.77; $0.14 per ADS) all down double-digits as well. Per the chart below, the bank's EPS performance was the worst in its peer group:

Net interest income accounts for roughly 75% of Handelsbanken's revenue, the highest mix in its peer set. At SEK 10.7 billion, this line was around 9% lower year-on-year and 6% sequentially, driven by a contracting net interest margin.

Handelsbanken's loan book skews heavily to Swedish real estate lending, particularly residential mortgages. These, in turn, skew more to floating rate than fixed rate. While that was a great when interest rates were rising, it is weighing now that they are declining, with the Riksbank's (Sweden's central bank) policy rate ending the quarter at 2%, down 200bps from last year's peak.

That being said, Handelsbanken's Q2 NII performance still looks poor. Indeed, the Riksbank only cut by 25bps last quarter, and that solitary cut came near the end of the period. In previous quarters, Handelsbanken was able to lower its deposit rates alongside interest rate cuts, dampening the overall impact on its NII. As last quarter was relatively flat from a rate perspective (just one cut right at the end), the bank's asset yields simply caught up a little more on the downswing, resulting in a relatively soft NII print.

Looking at the policy rate forecast above, you can see that this is seen at 1.88% next year, or 12bps below the prevailing rate. Since this is halfway to 25bps, it basically implies around a 50% chance of another rate cut. That means we could be near the end of the easing cycle and a bottom for the bank's NII.

Elsewhere, net fee and commission income was also pretty soft, declining ~2% year-on-year and ~1% sequentially to SEK 2.87 billion. The largest component of this revenue comes from mutual fund fees. At SEK 1.4 billion, this line fell ~7% year-on-year and ~4% quarter-on-quarter.

Management attributed this performance to weak stock markets, which makes sense. Since these fees are booked with a lag, they largely reflect what happened earlier in the year, when there was a large sell-off due to tariff fears. On top of that, Sweden's currency, the krona, also appreciated strongly, which would also have had an impact. The good news is that equity markets have since rebounded strongly, and this should result in stronger mutual fund income in Q3.

Finally, trading income was a significant negative contributor last quarter, declining by over SEK 600 million year-on-year. The good news (if you can call it that) is that this was mainly due to fair value changes in derivatives that Handelsbanken employs to manage various risks. While this can be volatile in any given quarter, it will basically net out to zero over time, as shown below. The important point is that customer-related trading income was stable. Accordingly, I would not extrapolate or read too much into this performance.

Costs were again a positive contributor. As I have mentioned before, Handelsbanken has been undergoing an efficiency drive, as the bank is exiting a period of elevated investment spending in areas like IT. That is allowing it to reduce headcount, particularly among external consultants, aiding its overall cost inflation. With that, total expenses came in at SEK 6.02 billion last quarter, down around 6% year-on-year, helped by lower staffing and IT-related costs.

Credit costs were likewise a positive. The bank booked a release for the sixth straight quarter, with management releasing the last of the macro add-ons it first laid on during COVID. Asset quality remains robust, with Stage 3 loans (non-performing) falling in absolute terms and relative to total balances. Given its prudent underwriting culture and the highly collateralized nature of its loan book (mostly residential and commercial real estate), low impairment charges have long been a feature of Handelsbanken. That said, credit costs will eventually normalize downwards, so this line won't pad earnings forever.

The Valuation

In Stockholm trading, Handelsbanken trades for around SEK 115 as I type (equal to around $5.95 per ADS), down from SEK 128 when I last covered it. That leaves the shares looking pretty cheap. Indeed, while Q2 results may have disappointed the market, the bank has still accrued SEK 7.15 per share in dividends linked to its H1 2025 performance. Annualized, that would put the forward dividend yield at over 12%.

There are a couple of points I would make about this. Firstly, Q2 was not a great one by Handelsbanken's standards, with the bank earning a 12.7% return on equity. That is a little below its long run average, and like I said, at least part of last quarter's performance was due to unrepresentative factors (trading income, for example). Still, annualizing last quarter's EPS would result in a price-to-earnings ratio of 10.4x, which looks quite cheap given it is based on depressed earnings.

The second point I would make concerns the bank's capital cushion. Handelsbanken is still sitting on a comfortable surplus, with its Common Equity Tier 1 ratio clocking in at 18.4% at the end of Q2. That is 50bps, or roughly SEK 4 billion, above the high-end of management's target range, which is further equal to circa ~2% of the bank's current market-cap. At the mid-point, surplus capital would be more like 5% of its current market-cap. Management can use this to support the dividend.

Conclusion

While they clearly disappointed the market, Svenska Handelsbanken's Q2 2025 results weren't quite as bad as they first appear, with an unrepresentative trading result driving a large part of the performance. Down around 8% since my last piece, these shares now offer investors a clear double-digit dividend yield, and that alone can drive an attractive total return. Accordingly, I remain bullish.
